name: Schumann resonance
note: The Earth–ionosphere cavity ringing near 7.83 Hz — the planet's natural EM heartbeat. The fundamental drifts and broadens with the day/night ionosphere (≈7.5–8.3 Hz); higher modes ring at 14, 20, 27 Hz.

name: AC mains
note: Power-line hum — the 60 Hz that runs North America (50 Hz across much of the world). The grid is held to ≈±0.05 Hz under normal conditions, wandering within that window minute to minute.

name: Navy ELF
note: US Navy ELF — 76 Hz signalling that reaches submerged submarines. Counter-intuitively narrow: at ~1 bit/minute the emission occupies only a few Hz, even though the ELF band itself spans 3 Hz–3 kHz.

name: VLF time/nav
note: Very low frequency (24 kHz) — global time/sync broadcasts that penetrate seawater to subs.

name: WWVB clock
note: WWVB 60 kHz time code — what 'atomic' wall clocks quietly sync to overnight.

name: LF RFID
note: Low-frequency RFID (125 kHz) — animal microchips, access fobs, immobilisers.

name: 2200 m
note: 2200 m — LF experimental; 1 W EIRP, CW & narrowband digital only.

name: Longwave radio
note: Longwave broadcast (153–279 kHz) — continent-spanning AM heard across Europe & Asia.

name: Aviation NDB
note: Non-directional beacons (LF) — among the oldest radio navaids still in service.

name: 630 m
note: 630 m — MF band shared with maritime; 5 W EIRP.

name: NAVTEX
note: NAVTEX — maritime safety, weather & navigation warnings to ships (518 kHz).

name: AM radio
note: AM broadcast. Amplitude-modulated; long groundwave range at night.

name: 160 m
note: 160 m 'Top Band' — night-time DX; General and up, all modes.

name: 80 m
note: 80/75 m — Technician CW window, voice for General and up; classic nighttime band.

name: 60 m
note: 60 m — five fixed USB channels; 100 W ERP, General and up.

name: Shortwave 49 m
note: Shortwave broadcast 49 m band — international radio that skips around the globe at night.

name: 40 m
note: 40 m — reliable day and night; Technician CW, voice for General and up.

name: WWV time signal
note: NIST WWV time & frequency standard — atomic-clock ticks at 2.5/5/10/15/20 MHz.

name: 30 m
note: 30 m WARC — CW and data only; 200 W PEP, no voice.

name: NFC / HF RFID
note: NFC & HF RFID (13.56 MHz ISM) — tap-to-pay, transit cards, smart labels.

name: 20 m ham
note: Premier HF DX band. CW, digital and SSB phone segments by licence class.

name: 17 m
note: 17 m WARC — CW/data plus voice for General and up; smooth DX band.

name: 15 m
note: 15 m — daytime DX in solar max; Technician CW, voice for General and up.

name: 12 m
note: 12 m WARC — CW/data plus voice; opens near the solar peak.

name: CB radio
note: Citizens Band — license-free voice, 40 channels, 4 W.

name: 10 m
note: 10 m — Technician voice at 28.3–28.5 MHz; spectacular in solar max.

name: 6 m
note: 6 m 'Magic Band' — sporadic-E surprises; all modes for Technician and up.

name: MRI RF
note: The RF pulse that flips proton spins in an MRI scanner. Its frequency is the Larmor frequency, set by the magnet's field: ≈8.5 MHz at 0.2 T, 64 MHz at 1.5 T, 128 MHz at 3 T (research 7 T scanners reach ~298 MHz). Any single scan's own bandwidth is just a few kHz.

name: VHF TV (low)
note: Low-VHF television — channels 2–6 (54–88 MHz). Sits just below the FM band; the two don't overlap.

name: FM radio
note: FM broadcast. Wideband stereo, line-of-sight propagation.

name: VOR / aviation nav
note: VOR & ILS — aircraft course beacons (108–118 MHz).

name: Air-traffic voice
note: VHF airband — air-traffic-control voice, 118–137 MHz AM.

name: NOAA wx sat
note: Polar weather-satellite image downlink (137 MHz APT) — receivable with a simple antenna.

name: 2 m ham
note: Local repeater / simplex band. Technician class and up.

name: MURS
note: Multi-Use Radio Service — five license-free VHF channels, 2 W: three at 151.82–151.94 MHz and two at 154.57–154.60 MHz. The ~2.6 MHz between them is other VHF land-mobile, not MURS. Like FRS but lower in frequency, with better outdoor range.

name: VHF land mobile
note: VHF business & public-safety two-way radio — the licensed land-mobile pool (utilities, security, local government, transport) that fills the VHF high band, interleaved with MURS and just below the marine channels.

name: Marine VHF
note: Marine VHF — ship-to-ship & coast-guard; channel 16 (156.8 MHz) is the distress call.

name: AIS ship tracking
note: AIS — automatic ship-position beacons (161.975 / 162.025 MHz).

name: NOAA Weather Radio
note: NOAA Weather Radio — continuous forecasts & alerts, 162.40–162.55 MHz.

name: VHF TV (high)
note: High-VHF television — channels 7–13 (174–216 MHz). The gap below it (108–174 MHz) holds FM, airband and VHF land-mobile, so VHF TV never overlaps FM.

name: 1.25 m
note: 1.25 m (222 MHz) — FM repeaters and weak-signal work, all modes.

name: Key fobs (315)
note: Car key fobs & garage remotes (US) — 315 MHz ISM.

name: Military UHF
note: Military UHF aircraft & satellite comms — 225–400 MHz.

name: Weather balloons
note: Radiosondes — weather-balloon telemetry at 403 MHz (meteorological aids).

name: Emergency beacons
note: EPIRB / PLB distress beacons — 406 MHz, relayed by Cospas-Sarsat search-and-rescue sats.

name: 433 MHz ISM
note: 433 MHz ISM — car fobs (EU), weather stations, remote switches, LPD radios.

name: 70 cm
note: 70 cm (440 MHz) — repeaters, satellites, and fast-scan TV.

name: UHF land mobile
note: UHF business & public-safety two-way radio (450–470 MHz) — the licensed land-mobile pool (utilities, security, taxis, local government) that fills the gap between the walkie-talkie channel groups.

name: Walkie-talkie
note: FRS & GMRS bubble-pack walkie-talkies — two channel groups, at 462 MHz (channels 1–7, 15–22) and 467 MHz (channels 8–14); the 463–467 MHz between them is other UHF land-mobile, not walkie-talkie. FRS is license-free (up to 2 W). A $35 GMRS licence (no exam, 10 years, whole family) buys high power (up to 50 W) on channels 1–7 and 15–22 — but on the *same* channels FRS uses, so a GMRS radio can drown out unlicensed users there. The real GMRS payoff is the 8 repeater-input channels (467.55–467.725 MHz, shown in purple), which FRS may not use: pairing with a hilltop repeater extends range far beyond simplex. An amateur (ham) licence does NOT cover GMRS — separate services (FCC Part 95 vs Part 97), so a Technician still buys the GMRS permit.

name: TV (UHF)
note: Digital TV broadcast (ATSC) — UHF channels 14–36 (470–608 MHz). The old top end (ch 38–51, 614–698 MHz) was reclaimed in the 2017 "repack" and handed to 600 MHz 5G, so UHF TV no longer reaches up into the cellular band.

name: 5G low-band
note: 600 MHz 5G/4G (n71) — repacked from TV; reaches deep into buildings and rural areas.

name: 700 MHz LTE
note: 700 MHz LTE (Bands 12/13) — strong coverage and building penetration.

name: FirstNet
note: FirstNet — nationwide public-safety LTE (Band 14, 700 MHz).

name: 850 MHz cellular
note: 850 MHz cellular (Band 5) — legacy CDMA/GSM voice, wide coverage.

name: 915 MHz ISM
note: 902–928 MHz ISM — LoRa, Z-Wave, smart meters, baby monitors, cordless phones.

name: 33 cm
note: 33 cm — shared with the 915 MHz ISM band; all modes for Technician and up.

name: ADS-B UAT
note: ADS-B UAT (978 MHz) — general-aviation position broadcast (US).

name: DME
note: DME — aircraft distance-measuring equipment (960–1215 MHz).

name: ADS-B
note: Aircraft position broadcast (1090 MHz). Trackable with a cheap SDR dongle.

name: GPS L5
note: GPS L5 — modern aviation-safety GNSS signal (1176.45 MHz).

name: GPS L2
note: GPS L2 — second GNSS frequency (1227.6 MHz), dual-band precision.

name: 23 cm
note: 23 cm (1.2 GHz) — ATV, satellites, and EME for Technician and up.

name: Hydrogen line (21 cm)
note: The 21 cm hydrogen line (1420.4 MHz) — neutral-hydrogen emission; cornerstone of radio astronomy & SETI.

name: Inmarsat MSS
note: Inmarsat / mobile-satellite service — L-band sat phones & data (~1.5 GHz).

name: GPS L1
note: GNSS positioning. Spread-spectrum, ~−130 dBm at the ground.

name: GLONASS
note: GLONASS L1 — the Russian GNSS constellation (≈1602 MHz).

name: GOES sat
note: Geostationary weather-satellite imagery downlink (1.69 GHz).

name: DECT cordless
note: DECT cordless phones — 1.88–1.90 GHz.

name: AWS cellular
note: AWS cellular (1.7/2.1 GHz, Band 66) — a 4G/5G capacity workhorse.

name: 13 cm
note: 13 cm (2.3 GHz) — weak-signal and ATV; shared with Wi-Fi and ISM.

name: Satellite radio
note: Satellite radio (SiriusXM) — S-band downlink, 2.32–2.345 GHz.

name: Bluetooth / Zigbee
note: Bluetooth & Zigbee — 2.4 GHz ISM, frequency-hopping personal-area links.

name: Wi-Fi 2.4
note: Shared ISM band: Wi-Fi ch 1/6/11, Bluetooth, Zigbee, microwave ovens.

name: 2.5 GHz 5G
note: 2.5 GHz 5G mid-band (n41) — a major capacity layer.

name: NEXRAD weather radar
note: NEXRAD Doppler weather radar (S-band, 2.7–3.0 GHz) — the national storm-tracking network.

name: 5G n77
note: 5G mid-band — the capacity workhorse for modern cellular.

name: C-band satellite
note: C-band satellite downlink (3.7–4.2 GHz) — TV distribution; lower edge repurposed for 5G.

name: Wi-Fi 5
note: 5 GHz Wi-Fi — more channels, shorter range than 2.4 GHz.

name: 5 cm
note: 5 cm (5.7 GHz) — microwave weak-signal and amateur TV.

name: Wi-Fi 6E / 7
note: Wi-Fi 6E/7 — the new 6 GHz band (5.925–7.125 GHz), wide and uncongested.

name: Wx radar
note: Weather / marine radar (X-band, 9.4 GHz). Pulsed, high peak power.

name: 3 cm
note: 3 cm (10 GHz) — microwave DX, EME, and narrowband experiments.

name: Ku-band satellite TV
note: Ku-band direct-broadcast satellite TV (12.2–12.7 GHz) — DirecTV / Dish.

name: Water maser (22 GHz)
note: The 22.235 GHz water-vapour maser — radio astronomy of star-forming regions and galactic nuclei.

name: 5G mmWave
note: 5G mmWave (n261, 28 GHz) — enormous capacity over a single city block.

name: Ka-band satellite
note: Ka-band satellite (20/30 GHz) — high-throughput broadband (incl. modern LEO mega-constellations).

name: WiGig 60
note: 60 GHz WiGig (802.11ad) — multi-gigabit links that oxygen absorption stops at the wall.

name: Automotive radar
note: Automotive radar (76–81 GHz) — adaptive cruise control & collision avoidance.

name: Cosmic microwave bg
note: The 2.7 K afterglow of the Big Bang — its blackbody spectrum peaks near 160 GHz.

name: Terahertz
note: Terahertz 'T-rays' (1 THz) — airport body scanners and the band science learned to use last.

name: CO₂ laser
note: CO₂ laser — industrial cutting & welding, laser surgery. Not a single line: it lases across a comb of rotational transitions spanning 9.2–10.8 µm (the 10.6 µm line is just the strongest).

name: IR heat
note: Thermal infrared (≈30 THz) — heat lamps, night vision, TV remotes nearby.

name: Fibre-optic telecom
note: Near-infrared (1550 nm) — the light that carries the internet through fibre under the oceans.

name: Fibre O-band
note: Fibre-optic O-band (1310 nm) — short-haul datacenter and access links.

name: Near-IR (940 nm)
note: Near-infrared (940 nm) — the invisible light a TV remote blinks. Also IR illuminators on security cameras, face-unlock dot projectors, and proximity sensors. The LED emission is genuinely broad (≈915–965 nm).

name: LIDAR
note: LIDAR — pulsed near-infrared light (commonly 905 nm; some automotive units use 1550 nm) for self-driving perception and 3-D mapping. Note it's *light*, not the 77 GHz radio of automotive radar.

name: 532 nm green laser
note: 532 nm green laser — pointers, rangefinders, and laser light shows. Frequency-doubled Nd:YAG; consumer units hold ±1–2 nm.

name: UV-A / blacklight
note: UV-A (315–400 nm) — the blacklight in a 365 nm torch that makes posters and tonic water glow, plus gel-nail/ink curing and most of a suntan. The everyday face of ultraviolet, and a physical-science band in its own right.

name: UV-B
note: UV-B (280–315 nm) — drives sunburn and vitamin-D synthesis; mostly absorbed by ozone.

name: UV-C
note: Germicidal ultraviolet (≈1 PHz); also the cause of sunburn at longer UV.

name: Lyman-alpha
note: Hydrogen Lyman-alpha (121.6 nm) — a key UV line for observing the Sun and the early universe.

name: EUV lithography
note: Extreme-UV (13.5 nm) — the tin-plasma light that prints today's microchips.

name: Soft X-rays
note: Soft X-rays (~1 keV) — solar-corona imaging and water-window microscopy.

name: X-ray crystallography
note: Cu Kα X-rays (8 keV) — how we read the atomic structure of proteins and DNA.

name: Medical X-ray
note: Diagnostic radiography & CT (≈3 EHz). Ionising; tightly dose-controlled.

name: Technetium-99m
note: Technetium-99m (140 keV) — the workhorse isotope of nuclear-medicine SPECT imaging.

name: PET annihilation
note: Positron-annihilation γ-rays (511 keV) — the photons a PET scanner maps to find tumours.

name: Caesium-137
note: Caesium-137 (662 keV) — calibration sources, food irradiation, and a fallout tracer.

name: Cobalt-60
note: Co-60 γ-rays — sterilising medical kit and treating cancer. Each decay emits two gammas, at 1.17 and 1.33 MeV, so it's really a pair of lines rather than one.

name: Cosmic γ-rays
note: High-energy cosmic γ-rays (~400 MeV) — light from pulsars, blazars and supernova remnants.
